summary: DDD starts by locating valuable business problems and model boundaries before choosing implementation patterns.
DDD tactical patterns are selected after the business boundary and model complexity are understood.
Strategic design begins with the business domain rather than technology structure.
Core subdomains are the differentiating subset of a business domain and deserve disproportionate modeling care.
Supporting subdomains matter to operations but usually do not justify the same investment as core differentiators.
Generic subdomains are common capabilities where reuse, purchase, or commodity implementation is often the rational choice.
Core domains are the strongest candidates for richer domain models because the business payoff can justify the extra design work.
Commodity logic often benefits more from simple procedural implementation than from a rich model.
Supporting CRUD-heavy domains may fit data-centric patterns when invariants are modest.
Domain experts supply the examples and distinctions needed to keep the shared language meaningful.
Raw business language becomes more useful when refined into a bounded, model-backed ubiquitous language.
A ubiquitous language is reliable only inside the boundary where its terms carry the same meaning.
Context maps make bounded contexts and their relationships visible for design and planning decisions.
Partnership is one context-map relationship when teams can coordinate closely.
Shared kernel is a context-map relationship that shares a small model subset under strict coordination.
Conformist relationships describe downstream acceptance of upstream model decisions.
An anticorruption layer appears on a context map where a local model needs protection from an external model.
Open host service is a context-map integration pattern where an upstream provides a stable public interface.
Shared kernels weaken context independence unless the shared part stays intentionally small and governed.
An anticorruption layer protects bounded context language and invariants from external model drift.
Simple procedural logic can move toward active record when data structures and persistence behavior need a modest home.
Active record becomes insufficient when behavior, invariants, and lifecycle rules need a richer model.
Entities model identity and lifecycle within a rich domain model.
Value objects make descriptive business values explicit without adding identity semantics.
Aggregates organize model objects around consistency boundaries and invariant enforcement.
Repositories separate aggregate retrieval and persistence from domain behavior.
Domain events expose meaningful facts that occurred inside the model without forcing direct coupling.
Repositories usually operate at aggregate boundaries rather than exposing arbitrary persistence internals.
Aggregates can publish domain events to communicate meaningful state changes outside their immediate transaction boundary.
Domain events become safer integration signals when their meaning is grounded in a bounded model.
Event sourcing relies on an append-only event store to retain event history as the source of state.
Event-sourced models commonly rebuild aggregate state from historical events.
CQRS often complements event sourcing because command history and query models have different optimization needs.
CQRS may use event-store data to build read models without forcing command storage and query storage into one shape.
Event sourcing is usually overkill for simple procedural domains where history is not central to the business model.
CQRS should not be introduced merely for structure when a simple write/read path is sufficient.
Event storming uses meaningful events to discover process flow, decisions, and hidden domain vocabulary.
Event storming can surface candidate aggregates after commands, policies, and invariants become visible.
Event storming helps test whether candidate context boundaries match actual business flows and language shifts.
Bounded contexts are a strong input for microservice boundaries, but deployment boundaries still require operational judgment.
Treating every bounded context as a service can create unnecessary distributed-system cost.
DDD boundaries clarify who owns events and what each event means across integration boundaries.
Event-driven integrations may still need translation boundaries when event meaning differs between contexts.
Domain boundaries can guide data product ownership in a data mesh architecture.
Useful domain-owned data products need language that analysts and operational teams interpret consistently.
Microservice decomposition should follow strategic understanding, not technology fashion or premature service slicing.
